interactions.summary: Jiang opens with a trick comparison between the battlefield genius and the person who only promotes the talented, then argues Robespierre is rarer and greater than Napoleon because selfless meritocracy violates ordinary family and social values.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture; audience prompts are rhetorical.

interactions.summary: He reconstructs Austerlitz as Napoleon baiting the coalition into attacking a weak right flank while relying on Davout to arrive from far away and save the plan.

claims.claim: Napoleon would not have been possible without Robespierre.
Robespierre is greater than Napoleon because promoting the talented requires selflessness and the capacity to ignore inherited social values.
Austerlitz made Napoleon legendary, but the plan depended on Davout arriving quickly enough to hold the right flank.

signature_moments.moment: The rarer genius is not the man with total battlefield awareness; it is the man who promotes the talented and gives up family, comfort, and social approval.
signature_moments.source_phrase: B is, I promote the talented. That's all B does.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This reversal sets the whole lecture against the conventional great-man reading of Napoleon.

signature_moments.moment: The Jack Ma inheritance thought experiment turns meritocracy into an act of almost inhuman self-denial.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Would there be anyone in China who would think I'm a good father? No one.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes Robespierre's virtue concrete instead of abstract.

signature_moments.moment: Austerlitz is presented as a perfect trap whose elegant design could collapse if one exhausted marshal arrived late.
signature_moments.source_phrase: It is perfect. Perfect timing. Perfect attack. Everything is perfect.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The example begins to separate Napoleon's vision from the system and officers that make it executable.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The ability to imagine a whole battlefield across nations, anticipate how battle will progress, and adjust with total battlefield awareness.
Napoleon's legendary 1805 battle, used here to show both his brilliance and his dependence on Davout and the revolutionary army.

chronology_notes.note: The lecture dates Austerlitz and the War of the Third Coalition to 1805 as the event that made Napoleon into a legend.

uncertainty_notes.note: Transcript renders Austerlitz as Auschwitz and Davout as De Vaux/the Vaux; the intended references are clear from context.
